draft.introduction: A meditation on God’s omniscience and omnipresence. The psalmist discovers there is nowhere to flee from God’s spirit—a text the Fathers used to argue for divine immanence.

translation.text: O Lord, you have searched me and known me! You know when I sit down and when I rise up; you discern my thoughts from afar. Where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence? If I ascend to heaven, you are there! If I make my bed in Sheol, you are there!
translation.original_text: κύριε ἐδοκίμασάς με καὶ ἔγνως με

translation.text: For you formed my inward parts; you knitted me together in my mother’s womb. I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. How precious to me are your thoughts, O God!
translation.original_text: ὅτι σὺ ἐκτήσω τοὺς νεφρούς μου

translator_notes.note: “Where shall I go from your Spirit?” (ποῦ πορευθῶ ἀπὸ τοῦ πνεύματός σου) was a key text for Basil of Caesarea’s pneumatology, arguing that the Spirit’s omnipresence proves divine status.
